prompt: Is there a relationship between professor hire date and their evaluation rating?

inputData.question: Is there a relationship between professor hire date and their evaluation rating?

If you prefer no minimum, remove that filter.","Professor rating is expected to be integer in the observed range 1-5 (confirmed column data_type is integer); we filter to that range.","Color/size encoding uses eval_count to show number of evaluations per professor; visualization consumer may map eval_count to point size or color.","Current date (current_date) is used to compute years since hire; replace with a reference date if a different time anchor is required."]},"attempt":2,"maxAttempts":4,"previousAttempt":{"sql":"SELECT p.id AS professor_id,
       p.hire_date AS hire_date,
       DATE_PART('year', AGE(current_date, p.hire_date))::integer AS years_since_hire,
       ROUND(AVG(ce.professor_rating)::numeric, 2) AS avg_professor_rating,
       COUNT(ce.id) AS eval_count
FROM public.professors p
JOIN public.course_sections cs ON cs.professor_id = p.id
JOIN public.enrollments e ON e.course_section_id = cs.id
JOIN public.course_evaluations ce ON ce.enrollment_id = e.id
WHERE p.hire_date IS NOT NULL
  AND ce.professor_rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5
GROUP BY p.id, p.hire_date
HAVING COUNT(ce.id) >= 5
ORDER BY years_since_hire ASC, professor_id ASC;","validationIssues":["Color and size both map to eval_count — redundant and may be visually confusing; consider using only one encoding (color or size) for eval_count.","You use years_since_hire (quantitative) rather than hire_date (temporal). This matches the plan, but if you intended a calendar-time analysis, put hire_date on a temporal axis instead.","metadata.rowCount shows a single sample row — verify the full query returns enough professors/points for a meaningful scatter plot."]}}},"startedAt":1771846950355,"status":"success","output":{"sql":"SELECT p.id AS professor_id,
       p.hire_date AS hire_date,
       DATE_PART('year', AGE(current_date, p.hire_date))::integer AS years_since_hire,
       ROUND(AVG(ce.professor_rating)::numeric, 2) AS avg_professor_rating,
       COUNT(ce.id) AS eval_count
FROM public.professors p
JOIN public.course_sections cs ON cs.professor_id = p.id
JOIN public.enrollments e ON e.course_section_id = cs.id
JOIN public.course_evaluations ce ON ce.enrollment_id = e.id
WHERE p.hire_date IS NOT NULL
  AND ce.professor_rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5

visualization.title: Professor average rating vs. years since hire

resultRaw.sql:
SELECT p.id AS professor_id,
       p.hire_date AS hire_date,
       DATE_PART('year', AGE(current_date, p.hire_date))::integer AS years_since_hire,
       ROUND(AVG(ce.professor_rating)::numeric, 2) AS avg_professor_rating,
       COUNT(ce.id) AS eval_count
FROM public.professors p
JOIN public.course_sections cs ON cs.professor_id = p.id
JOIN public.enrollments e ON e.course_section_id = cs.id
JOIN public.course_evaluations ce ON ce.enrollment_id = e.id
WHERE p.hire_date IS NOT NULL
  AND ce.professor_rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5
GROUP BY p.id, p.hire_date
HAVING COUNT(ce.id) >= 5
ORDER BY years_since_hire ASC, professor_id ASC;

issues: Color and size both map to eval_count — redundant and may be visually confusing; consider using only one encoding (color or size) for eval_count.
You use years_since_hire (quantitative) rather than hire_date (temporal). This matches the plan, but if you intended a calendar-time analysis, put hire_date on a temporal axis instead.
metadata.rowCount shows a single sample row — verify the full query returns enough professors/points for a meaningful scatter plot.

visualization.title: Professor average rating vs. years since hire

result.sql:
SELECT p.id AS professor_id,
       p.hire_date AS hire_date,
       DATE_PART('year', AGE(current_date, p.hire_date))::integer AS years_since_hire,
       ROUND(AVG(ce.professor_rating)::numeric, 2) AS avg_professor_rating,
       COUNT(ce.id) AS eval_count
FROM public.professors p
JOIN public.course_sections cs ON cs.professor_id = p.id
JOIN public.enrollments e ON e.course_section_id = cs.id
JOIN public.course_evaluations ce ON ce.enrollment_id = e.id
WHERE p.hire_date IS NOT NULL
  AND ce.professor_rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5
GROUP BY p.id, p.hire_date
HAVING COUNT(ce.id) >= 5
ORDER BY years_since_hire ASC, professor_id ASC;

issues: Color and size both map to eval_count — redundant and may be visually confusing; consider using only one encoding (color or size) for eval_count.
You use years_since_hire (quantitative) rather than hire_date (temporal). This matches the plan, but if you intended a calendar-time analysis, put hire_date on a temporal axis instead.
metadata.rowCount shows a single sample row — verify the full query returns enough professors/points for a meaningful scatter plot.
